skill.name: dask
skill.description: Guides Dask usage for scalable Python data processing with DataFrames, Arrays, Bags, Futures, and scheduler choices.
skill.summary: Use Dask to scale pandas, NumPy, and custom Python workflows beyond single-machine memory.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, filesystem, C2, reconnaissance, and weak crypto patterns. Manual review found these are Markdown documentation examples, API names, scheduler terms, and file path examples, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, exfiltration logic, install hook, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner interpreted Markdown backticks and Python code examples as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed locations are documentation for Dask APIs and are not executable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown prose, inline code formatting, or fenced Python examples. No shell command execution primitive or runnable wrapper file is present.

low_findings.title: High-Risk Keyword Detections Are Benign Dask Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: C2, reconnaissance, and weak cryptography detections matched words such as combine, dashboard, distributed, ThreadPoolExecutor, and file extensions. These terms describe Dask scheduling and aggregation concepts rather than malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious keywords appear inside ordinary Dask API explanations and performance guidance. No encoded payload, command channel, cryptographic implementation, or reconnaissance workflow was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Pattern Is a Local Data Loading Example
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding points to a glob pattern used in a Dask Bag example for reading local JSON files. It does not grant hidden file access or instruct the model to collect sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is an illustrative data ingestion pattern for user-provided files. There is no network sink, secret path targeting, or automated traversal behavior.

content.user_title: Scale Python Data Workflows with Dask
content.value_statement: Large Python datasets can exceed local memory and slow pandas or NumPy workflows. This skill guides Dask DataFrames, Arrays, Bags, Futures, and schedulers.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Dask DataFrames instead of pandas.
Guides Dask Array chunking for larger-than-memory NumPy workflows.
Shows how Dask Bags process text, JSON, logs, and Python objects.
Describes Futures for dynamic task submission and dependency handling.
Compares thread, process, synchronous, and distributed scheduler choices.
Provides performance guidance for partitions, chunks, persistence, and dashboards.
limitations: It does not install Dask or manage cluster infrastructure.
It cannot verify memory usage without access to runtime metrics.
It focuses on Dask concepts and examples, not every Dask extension.
It assumes users can adapt examples to their own data paths and schemas.

use_cases.title: Scale a pandas pipeline
use_cases.description: Convert a pandas workflow into a Dask DataFrame pipeline for larger CSV or Parquet datasets.

use_cases.title: Process scientific arrays
use_cases.description: Plan Dask Array chunking and compute steps for large numerical datasets.

use_cases.title: Build custom parallel tasks
use_cases.description: Use Dask Futures to submit dependent tasks and monitor execution on a local or distributed cluster.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right Dask collection
prompt_templates.prompt: I have data shaped like [describe data]. Help me choose between Dask DataFrame, Array, Bag, and Futures.

prompt_templates.title: Convert pandas to Dask
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pandas workflow and describe how to convert it to a Dask DataFrame workflow safely.

prompt_templates.title: Tune partitions and chunks
prompt_templates.prompt: Given my dataset size, memory, core count, and operations, recommend Dask partition or chunk settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design a distributed workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Dask Futures workflow for dependent tasks, data scattering, monitoring, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether to use pandas or Dask for many Parquet files larger than memory.
output_examples.output: The response recommends Dask DataFrames, explains lazy evaluation, suggests partition checks, and identifies when to persist intermediate results.

output_examples.input: A researcher has a large multidimensional array and slow NumPy operations.
output_examples.output: The response explains chunk sizing, warns about unsupported operations, and proposes a staged compute strategy.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to run many dependent Python tasks on a cluster.
output_examples.output: The response recommends Dask Futures, describes task submission, data scattering, result gathering, and dashboard monitoring.

best_practices: Load data with Dask readers instead of creating large local pandas or NumPy objects first.
Choose partitions or chunks that balance memory use with scheduler overhead.
Use the Dask dashboard and persist important intermediates when tuning performance.
anti_patterns: Calling compute repeatedly inside loops instead of computing related results together.
Creating millions of tiny tasks when partition-level work would be more efficient.
Using distributed scheduling before confirming that data size and workload justify it.

faq.question: Does this skill run Dask jobs for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for writing Dask workflows in your own environment.

faq.question: Can it help migrate pandas code?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Dask DataFrame patterns, lazy execution, partitioning, and common migration pitfalls.

faq.question: Does it cover distributed clusters?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes distributed clients, Futures, dashboards, and scheduler selection at a conceptual level.

faq.question: Is this only for tabular data?
faq.answer: No. It also covers arrays, unstructured data with Bags, and custom task workflows with Futures.

faq.question: Will it choose exact memory settings automatically?
faq.answer: No. It can recommend settings from supplied hardware and dataset details, but runtime validation is still needed.

faq.question: Is the marketplace version safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation-only content and no confirmed malicious behavior.
